message:
feat(stdlib): add fmtcheck (BSD printf-format compatibility check) (bd-hmwxy)

NetBSD/FreeBSD libutil fmtcheck(user, default_fmt) — verify that a
user-supplied printf format string declares the same sequence of
variadic conversion specifiers as a trusted default, returning the
user pointer when compatible and the default pointer when not.
Used by syslog wrappers and any tool that wants to safely accept a
user-supplied format string without buffer-overflow risk.

* core::stdlib::fmtcheck builds on the existing
  core::stdio::printf::parse_format_string parser. Each FormatSpec
  is normalized into a ConvShape enum capturing the type-class
  (signed int / unsigned int / float / char / string / pointer /
  store-count) and length-modifier class (default / short / long /
  long long / size / long double). %d ≡ %i, all unsigned families
  interchange, all float families interchange, but %d vs %ld remain
  distinct (different va_arg consumption widths). Literal text,
  flags, width, precision are ignored.
* stdlib_abi::fmtcheck shim handles NULL guards (NULL user →
  default; NULL default → NULL even if user is set, matching the
  BSD contract that callers assume default is always returnable as
  fallback) and otherwise delegates to core::compatible.

23 core unit tests covering: empty formats, every conversion
character, every length modifier, d/i interchange, unsigned-family
interchange, float interchange, length-mod sensitivity (d vs ld),
literal-text invariance, flag/width/precision invariance, char vs
int distinct, pointer vs string distinct + 12 abi integration
tests covering NULL guards, pointer-equality returns, every
classifier rule. Clippy-clean, fmt-clean. All builds via rch.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
